---
title: "Predicting Outpatient Consultation Duration"
subtitle: "Smarter Scheduling and Fairer Costing for an Outpatient Clinic"
author: "Ariana Butina"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    transition: slide
    incremental: false
    fig-width: 6.5
    fig-height: 4
    embed-resources: true
jupyter: python3
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
import json
from pathlib import Path

from IPython.display import Image

ARTIFACTS = Path("../ml/artifacts")


def load_json(name: str) -> dict:
    with open(ARTIFACTS / name) as f:
        return json.load(f)


clf_metrics = load_json("test_metrics_classification.json")
reg_metrics = load_json("test_metrics_regression.json")
```

## The Problem

- Clinics need to know **how long a visit will take** — both to build a workable
  schedule and to know what a visit actually costs to deliver.
- Today, most clinics use one generic appointment length for everyone.
- Result: some slots run short, others run long, and nobody can answer *"how much
  clinician time does a typical visit really need?"*

## Our Approach {.smaller}

- We used **two years of real visit records** from an outpatient oncology clinic
  (6,637 consultations).
- We built two simple tools, using only information already known when an appointment
  is booked (has the patient visited before? what day/time? is a diagnosis on file?):
  1. **A "long visit" flag** — will this visit likely run longer than usual?
  2. **A duration estimate** — roughly how many minutes will it take?
- Both are available through a simple web form for schedulers to try.

## What We Found

::: {layout-ncol="2"}
```{python}
#| label: fig-confusion-matrix
#| fig-cap: "Long-visit flag: correct calls vs. mistakes"
Image("figures/confusion_matrix.png")
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-residuals
#| fig-cap: "Duration estimate vs. actual visit length"
Image("figures/residuals.png")
```
:::

## What We Found (in Plain Terms)

- The "long visit" flag gets it right about **6 times out of 10** — better than a coin
  flip, not a certainty.
- The duration estimate is typically off by a little over **4 minutes** — good for
  planning, not for setting one exact slot.

## The Headline Finding

> **Whether a patient has visited before is, by far, the biggest driver of visit
> length — far more than the day of the week, the time of day, or the diagnosis on
> file.**

New and returning patients simply need different amounts of time, and that one fact
matters more than everything else combined.

## What This Means in Practice

- **Scheduling:** use these tools to plan overall daily/weekly capacity — not to fix
  the exact length of any single appointment.
- **Costing:** the clearest way to estimate "time per visit" for cost planning is to
  separate **new-patient visits from returning-patient visits** — not by day or
  diagnosis.
- **Next step:** capturing a bit more detail about visit complexity would likely make
  these estimates noticeably better.

## Try It Yourself

- **Live app:** [frontend-psi-seven-76.vercel.app](https://frontend-psi-seven-76.vercel.app)
- **Source code:** [github.com/arianabutina-source/hangu-consult-time-ml](https://github.com/arianabutina-source/hangu-consult-time-ml)

## Thank You

Everyday scheduling data is enough to make a genuinely useful — if imperfect —
prediction about visit length, and to point clearly toward a better way to estimate
clinic costs.

**Questions?**
